topic: Winterizing Pipes Las Vegas: Why a Desert City Still Busts Lines on Freeze Nights
excerpt: Las Vegas sees freezing temps with some regularity, and the damage usually hits garage walls, exterior hose bibs, backflow devices, attic runs, and cabinets on outside walls. The local soil profile (loamy, carbonatic soils with a petrocalcic “caliche” layer) matters because it often drives shallow/exposed routing for irrigation and exterior plumbing components—and those are the first to get nailed when the wind chills the wall. Seasonal behavior is predictable: the first real cold week is when we see surprise failures, and then the next day’s thaw is when leaks show up. Material stress is straight physics—water expands as it freezes—so prevention is about insulation, sealing drafts, controlled heat, and smart shutoffs.
markdown_tables:
| High-ticket winterization scope (Las Vegas) | What it usually includes | Typical total |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Exterior bib + backflow protection package | Covers/insulation, draft sealing, test/verify | $400–$1,200 |
| Garage + exterior wall line protection | Insulate runs, seal penetrations, cabinet airflow plan | $600–$1,800 |
| Whole-home freeze-prep (1-day) | All vulnerable lines, hose shutoffs, drip plan, shutoff location labeling | $1,200–$3,500 |
| Irrigation winter protection (system-dependent) | Drain/blowout where appropriate, backflow protection, controller settings | $500–$2,500 |
| Pool equipment freeze-prep | Protect exposed pipes/valves, circulation plan for freeze nights | $400–$1,800 |
| Smart leak protection upgrade | Leak sensors + automatic shutoff planning/installation | $800–$4,500 |
